---
title: "How mizerMR uses mizer's extension mechanism"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: yes
    fig_width: 5
    fig_height: 5
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{How mizerMR uses mizer's extension mechanism}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
library(mizerMR)
```

# Overview

This vignette is aimed at developers who want to understand how mizerMR is
built on top of mizer's extension machinery. It assumes you have read
`vignette("extensions", package = "mizer")`.

mizerMR uses four of the five extension routes described there:

1. **`setComponent()`** — the multiple resources are stored as a single
   dynamical component named `"MR"`.
2. **`setRateFunction()`** — encounter and resource-mortality rates are
   replaced with MR-aware versions.
3. **`other_params()`** — resource-level parameters (kappa, lambda, …) are
   stored alongside the component parameters.
4. **S3 subclassing** — `MRMizerParams` and `MRMizerSim` subclasses trigger
   automatic dispatch to mizerMR methods for plotting, accessors, and
   projection.

# The resource state lives in a `setComponent()` component

The multiple resources are stored as a single dynamical component named
`"MR"`. The component state is an array of dimensions `(resource × size)`
holding the number density of each resource at each size on mizer's full size
grid. `setMultipleResources()` registers this component:

```r
params <- setComponent(
    params      = params,
    component   = "MR",
    initial_value = initial_resource,   # array (resource × size)
    dynamics_fun  = "mizerMR_dynamics",
    component_params = list(
        rate        = resource_rate,        # array (resource × size)
        capacity    = resource_capacity,    # array (resource × size)
        interaction = resource_interaction  # matrix (species × resource)
    )
)
```

During a call to `project()`, mizer automatically calls `mizerMR_dynamics()` at
each time step. That function iterates over resources and applies semi-chemostat
dynamics to each row of the state array, using the per-resource mortality
computed earlier in the rate pipeline.

The component state at every saved time step ends up in `sim@n_other[, "MR"]`
and is exposed to users via `NResource(sim)` and `finalNResource(sim)`.

## Where parameters are stored

Per-resource scalar parameters (kappa, lambda, r_pp, w_min, w_max) are stored
as a data frame at `other_params(params)[["MR"]]$resource_params`. The
per-size arrays derived from those parameters live inside the component's own
`component_params`, which mizer stores under the same `other_params[["MR"]]`
key:

```
params@other_params[["MR"]]
  ├── resource_params  # data frame (resource × columns)
  ├── rate             # array (resource × size)
  ├── capacity         # array (resource × size)
  └── interaction      # matrix (species × resource)
```

# Two rate functions replace the defaults

## `mizerMREncounter`

Registered with:

```r
params <- setRateFunction(params, "Encounter", "mizerMREncounter")
```

Instead of convolving the predation kernel against a single `n_pp`, it first
multiplies the multi-resource state array by the species–resource interaction
matrix to form a weighted prey field, adds the fish-to-fish contribution, and
then runs the FFT-based convolution against the combined field. It also loops
over `params@other_encounter` to include encounter contributions from any
additional registered components — replicating work that the default
`mizerEncounter()` would otherwise do, but which no longer runs because the
entire encounter calculation has been replaced.

## `mizerMRResourceMort`

Registered with:

```r
params <- setRateFunction(params, "ResourceMort", "mizerMRResourceMort")
```

mizer's default `ResourceMort` returns a single vector (one value per size bin)
representing mortality on `n_pp`. The replacement returns a matrix
`(resource × size)` by multiplying the predation rate matrix by the
species–resource interaction:

```r
mizerMRResourceMort <- function(params, n, n_pp, n_other, t, pred_rate, ...) {
    t(params@other_params[["MR"]]$interaction) %*% pred_rate
}
```

This matrix is later passed row-by-row to each per-resource dynamics function
inside `mizerMR_dynamics()`.

## The built-in single resource is silenced

```r
mizer::initialNResource(params) <- 0         # abundance set to zero
resource_dynamics(params) <- "resource_constant"  # kept at zero forever
```

# S3 subclassing for automatic dispatch

mizerMR defines two formal S4 subclasses so that R's S3 dispatch routes
calls to the right method without any manual `if` guards:

```r
setClass("MRMizerParams", contains = "MizerParams")
setClass("MRMizerSim",    contains = "MizerSim")
```

## `setMultipleResources()` returns `MRMizerParams`

The last action of `setMultipleResources()` is:

```r
new("MRMizerParams", params)
```

Because `MRMizerParams` inherits all slots from `MizerParams` and adds none of
its own, this is a zero-cost wrapping step. Every existing mizer function that
accepts a `MizerParams` object continues to work on `MRMizerParams` without
any changes.

## `project.MRMizerParams` returns `MRMizerSim`

```r
project.MRMizerParams <- function(params, ...) {
    sim <- NextMethod()       # calls mizer's project()
    new("MRMizerSim", sim)   # wraps the result
}
```

This ensures that every simulation started from an `MRMizerParams` is
automatically an `MRMizerSim`, so the correct downstream methods fire without
the user doing anything special.

## S3 methods replace manual `if` guards

Before this refactor, every accessor and plot function contained an explicit
check:

```r
if (is.null(getComponent(params, "MR"))) {
    return(mizer::foo(params, ...))   # fall back to mizer
}
# ... MR-aware implementation ...
```

The same check was repeated in every function, the functions shadowed mizer's
namespace without being formal S3 methods, and there was no guarantee that the
two branches stayed consistent.

After the refactor each function is split into a method pair. For example,
`NResource`:

```r
# MR-aware method — no guard needed, called only when sim is MRMizerSim
NResource.MRMizerSim <- function(sim) {
    n_res <- aperm(simplify2array(NOther(sim)[, "MR"]), c(3, 1, 2))
    dimnames(n_res)[[1]] <- dimnames(NOther(sim))[[1]]
    names(dimnames(n_res))[[1]] <- names(dimnames(NOther(sim)))[[1]]
    n_res
}
```

When a user calls `NResource(sim)` and `sim` is an `MRMizerSim`, R dispatches
to `NResource.MRMizerSim`. When `sim` is a plain `MizerSim` (no multiple
resources), R dispatches to mizer's own method. No package-level override is
needed and the two branches are cleanly separated.

The full set of S3 methods defined by mizerMR is:

| Generic | Method class | Defined in |
|---|---|---|
| `project` | `MRMizerParams` | `project_methods.R` |
| `resource_params`, `resource_params<-` | `MRMizerParams` | `resource_params.R` |
| `resource_capacity`, `resource_capacity<-` | `MRMizerParams` | `setMultipleResources.R` |
| `resource_rate`, `resource_rate<-` | `MRMizerParams` | `setMultipleResources.R` |
| `resource_interaction`, `resource_interaction<-` | `MRMizerParams` | `setMultipleResources.R` |
| `initialNResource`, `initialNResource<-` | `MRMizerParams` | `setMultipleResources.R` |
| `NResource` | `MRMizerSim` | `NResource.R` |
| `finalNResource` | `MRMizerSim` | `NResource.R` |
| `getDiet` | `MRMizerParams` | `diet.R` |
| `plotDiet` | `MRMizerParams`, `MRMizerSim` | `diet.R` |
| `plotSpectra` | `MRMizerParams`, `MRMizerSim` | `plotSpectra.R` |
| `animateSpectra` | `MRMizerSim` | `animateSpectra.R` |
| `plotResourceLevel` | `MRMizerParams`, `MizerParams` | `plotResource.R` |
| `plotResourcePred` | `MRMizerParams`, `MizerParams` | `plotResource.R` |

## Avoiding dispatch loops when calling mizer's own functions

Several mizerMR methods (e.g. `plotSpectra.MRMizerParams`) need to call mizer's
version of the same generic to get species-only data. Calling
`mizer::plotSpectra(params, ...)` with `params` still being an `MRMizerParams`
would loop back to our method because `mizer::plotSpectra` is itself just the
S3 generic. The solution is to downcast `params` before the call:

```r
df <- mizer::plotSpectra(as(params, "MizerParams"),
                         resource = FALSE, return_data = TRUE, ...)
```

`as(params, "MizerParams")` strips the subclass, so mizer's
`plotSpectra.MizerParams` is dispatched rather than ours.

## `MRMizerSim` plot methods delegate to the params method

When a plot function is called with a simulation object, it needs to collapse
the time-averaged abundances back into an `MRMizerParams`. The `MRMizerSim`
methods do this uniformly:

```r
plotSpectra.MRMizerSim <- function(object, ...) {
    params <- new("MRMizerParams", setInitialValues(object@params, object))
    plotSpectra(params, ...)
}
```

`setInitialValues()` is a mizer function that copies the final (or
time-averaged) abundances into the initial-value slots of the params. It
returns a plain `MizerParams`, so we re-wrap with `new("MRMizerParams", ...)`
before dispatching onward.

# Summary of benefits

| Aspect | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Dispatch logic | `is.null(getComponent(...))` guard in every function | R method dispatch — no guards |
| Namespace | Functions shadowed mizer's generics | Proper S3 methods alongside mizer's |
| Fallback to mizer | Explicit `mizer::foo()` call inside the guard | Automatic — non-MR objects dispatch to mizer |
| Extensibility | Third-party code must call `mizerMR::foo()` explicitly | Standard R generics work transparently |
| Correctness risk | Guard condition must be kept consistent everywhere | Enforced by class membership |
| Bugs fixed | `plotResourcePred`/`plotResourceLevel` used wrong `other_params` path | Replaced by proper accessor functions |
